## Human
*2026-04-03 22:11:51 UTC*

ok. while this is running, here's a recent conversation i had with claude.ai:

```
I have a question for you -- is it actually realistic to get the AI to do a loop like that?Show less6:03 PMEvaluated practical feasibility of iterative AI-driven requirement loopsEvaluated practical feasibility of iterative AI-driven requirement loopsYes, but the loop has to be external to the model, not internal. A model can't reliably loop within a single prompt — it'll decide it's done based on vibes, not coverage. But a shell script that runs a coverage check, identifies gaps, generates a focused prompt for the gaps, runs it, and repeats — that's just the same Copilot CLI automation you've been using all along. Each iteration is a fresh prompt with a specific task: "these 12 files have no requirements, write requirements for them." The loop termination is a deterministic check (no uncovered files with behavioral contracts), not the model deciding it's finished.
The risk isn't the loop mechanism — it's diminishing returns. The first pass gets the obvious stuff (70 requirements). The gap audit gets the domain knowledge stuff (7 more). The coverage-map pass will get the files that were simply never looked at (date adapters, enum handling, array adapters — probably another 30-40). But after that, you're into increasingly marginal territory where each iteration produces 2-3 requirements that are increasingly vague or redundant. You need a stopping criterion that isn't just "no uncovered files" but "no uncovered files with nontrivial behavioral contracts." A utility class with no public API doesn't need requirements.
The other practical consideration: each iteration costs one premium request and 2-3 minutes. If convergence takes 5-6 iterations, that's 15 minutes and 6 requests to go from 77 requirements to maybe 150. That's a good trade. If it takes 15 iterations because the model keeps finding one more file to write a vague requirement for, the stopping criterion is too loose.
Cowork's approach of using file coverage as the mechanical check is right. The refinement I'd add: after the first coverage pass fills the obvious gaps, switch the check from "which files have no requirements" to "which requirements have no conditions of satisfaction that could generate a failing test." That catches the difference between a requirement that says something testable and one that's just restating what the code does.Ok, but this isn't actually running in a shell script -- that's just a testing mechanism. This is running as a skill in Copilot or Claude Code.6:04 PMReconciled agent-based looping with practical constraints and context limitsReconciled agent-based looping with practical constraints and context limitsRight, that changes the answer. The agent is the loop. Claude Code and Copilot agents already have a built-in loop — they read files, run commands, check results, decide what to do next, and keep going. The skill just needs to tell the agent to do the coverage check as part of Step 7 rather than leaving "am I done?" as a judgment call.
The instruction in the SKILL.md would be something like: "After generating requirements, list all source files with public API surface. Check which files are referenced in at least one requirement. For any file with behavioral contracts that has zero requirement coverage, generate requirements for it. Repeat until no significant uncovered files remain."
The agent will do that naturally — it'll grep, find the gaps, write more requirements, grep again. That's just tool use within a single session. No shell script needed.
The practical limits are context window and session length, not capability. After generating 150 requirements with the full template (summary, user story, implementation note, conditions of satisfaction, alternative paths, references), you're consuming a lot of context. The agent might need to write requirements to the file as it goes rather than holding everything in memory, and it might need to read the file back to check coverage rather than relying on what it remembers writing.
The other limit is that the agent has to be able to distinguish "this file has behavioral contracts worth testing" from "this is an internal utility with no public API." The stopping criterion in the skill needs to say something like "stop when remaining uncovered files are internal implementation details with no user-facing behavioral contracts" — otherwise it'll keep going forever writing requirements for every helper class.
But yes, this is exactly what agentic coding tools are built to do. The skill tells the agent the process, including the loop and the termination criterion, and the agent executes it with its own tool-use cycle. That's more natural than a shell script and it's how the playbook would actually be used.Can we alleviate the context window issue by having it keep some of the context in files?6:05 PMExamined file-based context offloading for extended agent sessionsExamined file-based context offloading for extended agent sessionsThat's exactly the right approach, and it's what the playbook should already be doing. The agent writes requirements to REQUIREMENTS.md as it goes, writes the coverage map to a tracking file, and reads them back when it needs to check progress. The context window only needs to hold the current iteration's work — the accumulated state lives on disk.
The pattern would be: generate requirements for subsystem 1, write them to REQUIREMENTS.md, move on. Generate requirements for subsystem 2, append to REQUIREMENTS.md, move on. When it's time to do the coverage check, read the file list, grep REQUIREMENTS.md for references, write the gap list to a coverage file, then generate requirements for the gaps. At no point does the agent need to hold 150 requirements in context simultaneously — it just needs to hold the current batch plus the coverage gap list.
This is also how a human quality engineer works. You don't keep the entire requirements document in your head. You write it down, move to the next section, and refer back to the document when you need to check coverage. The file system is the external memory.
The skill instruction for this would be something like: "Write requirements to quality/REQUIREMENTS.md incrementally by subsystem. After each subsystem, append to the file rather than rewriting it. Use quality/coverage_check.md as a working file to track which source files have requirements coverage and which don't. Read these files back when you need to assess progress."
The one thing to be explicit about in the skill: tell the agent not to rewrite the entire REQUIREMENTS.md on each iteration. Append new requirements, don't regenerate old ones. Otherwise the agent will try to be helpful by rewriting the whole file with "improvements," which burns context and risks losing earlier work.here's what it responded with:The problem is attention, not capability. The model *knows* about date/time handling and enum contracts and array edge cases — it just runs out of steam after writing 70 requirements and decides it's done. Telling it "keep going" in a single prompt won't fix that because it's a context/fatigue issue, not an instruction-following issue.

The fix is to make "am I done?" a mechanical check rather than a judgment call. Here's the approach:

**Step 1: Source file coverage map.** This is deterministic — grep REQUIREMENTS.md for references to each source file and find the ones with zero hits. No model judgment needed.

**Step 2: Generate requirements for uncovered files.** A focused prompt that says "here are the files with no requirements, write requirements for them."

**Step 3: Repeat until stable.** The loop terminates when the coverage check finds no significant uncovered files.

Let me build it:Good — the grep-in-subshell is mangling the output but the data is there. Let me write a cleaner version as a Python script:34 uncovered files totaling 5,720 lines. That's a lot of behavioral surface with zero requirements. Now let me create the gap-fill prompt and the loop runner:Now the loop runner that ties it all together:Here's what I built. Three pieces:

**`check_coverage.py`** — the mechanical termination check. Greps REQUIREMENTS.md for every source file's class name, reports which files have zero references. Exits 0 when all significant files (≥50 lines) are covered, exits 1 when there are gaps. Writes the uncovered file list to `uncovered_files.txt` for the next step to consume.

**`prompt_requirements_gap_fill.md`** — reads the uncovered file list, reads each actual source file, and writes requirements for them. Groups related files into subsystem sections (date/time, tree model bridge, enum, arrays/collections, parsing entry points, numeric handling, naming/field metadata). Appends to the existing REQUIREMENTS.md without touching what's already there.

**`run_requirements_loop.sh`** — the loop. Runs `check_coverage.py` → if gaps exist, runs the gap-fill prompt → runs coverage check again → repeats. Stops when either: all significant files are covered, no new requirements were added (stuck), or 5 iterations hit (safety cap). Logs everything.

The current state: 77 requirements covering 38 of 81 source files. 34 significant files uncovered, totaling 5,720 lines. The biggest gaps are exactly what you and Claude.ai identified — JavaTimeTypeAdapters (449 lines), JsonTreeReader (391 lines), ISO8601Utils (386 lines), DefaultDateTypeAdapter (198 lines), EnumTypeAdapter (129 lines), ArrayTypeAdapter (112 lines).

To run it:

```bash
cd /path/to/gson-1.2.15
bash control_prompts/run_requirements_loop.shIt'll probably do 2-3 iterations. The first pass should cover most of the 34 files. The second pass picks up anything the first pass missed or any new files that become significant. The third pass should find nothing and stop.
```should i give it a follow-up to ask it about context window management?Show more6:07 PMAssessed whether context management guidance needed for skill implementationAssessed whether context management guidance needed for skill implementationNot yet — look at what Cowork built. The loop is already a shell script that runs separate Copilot CLI calls per iteration. Each iteration is a fresh context. The coverage check writes gaps to uncovered_files.txt, the next prompt reads that file, appends new requirements to REQUIREMENTS.md, and exits. The file-based state management is already happening naturally because each iteration is an independent API call.
The context window conversation becomes relevant when you bake this into the SKILL.md as a single-session agent workflow instead of a shell script loop. That's the translation step — turning run_requirements_loop.sh into Step 7 instructions that an agent follows within one session. That's when you need to tell it to write incrementally and read back from the file. But for right now, let it run as-is and see whether the loop converges and what quality of requirements it produces for the 34 uncovered files. The mechanism question is settled — the content quality question is what matters next.
```

**`run_requirements_loop.sh`** — the loop. Runs `check_coverage.py` → if gaps exist, runs the gap-fill prompt → runs coverage check again → repeats. Stops when either: all significant files are covered, no new requirements were added (stuck), or 5 iterations hit (safety cap). Logs everything.

The current state: 77 requirements covering 38 of 81 source files. 34 significant files uncovered, totaling 5,720 lines. The biggest gaps are exactly what you and Claude.ai identified — JavaTimeTypeAdapters (449 lines), JsonTreeReader (391 lines), ISO8601Utils (386 lines), DefaultDateTypeAdapter (198 lines), EnumTypeAdapter (129 lines), ArrayTypeAdapter (112 lines).

To run it:

```bash
cd /path/to/gson-1.2.15
bash control_prompts/run_requirements_loop.shIt'll probably do 2-3 iterations. The first pass should cover most of the 34 files. The second pass picks up anything the first pass missed or any new files that become significant. The third pass should find nothing and stop.

The coverage check writes gaps to uncovered_files.txt, the next prompt reads that file, appends new requirements to REQUIREMENTS.md, and exits. The file-based state management is already happening naturally because each iteration is an independent API call.
The context window conversation becomes relevant when you bake this into the SKILL.md as a single-session agent workflow instead of a shell script loop. ## Assistant
*2026-04-03 22:12:00 UTC*

Claude.ai's analysis is right on two points and wrong on one.

**Right: the shell script is a testing mechanism, not the final form.** The goal is to bake this into the SKILL.md so that when an agent runs Step 7, it does the coverage loop itself within a single session. The shell script proves the approach works and calibrates the stopping criterion. Once we know how many iterations it takes and what quality of output each produces, we write the instructions.

**Right: file-based state is already happening.** Each Copilot CLI call in the loop is a fresh context that reads `REQUIREMENTS.md` and `uncovered_files.txt` from disk. That's the same pattern a Claude Code or Copilot agent would use within a single session — write to the file, read it back, check coverage, keep going. The translation from shell script to SKILL.md instructions is mostly just describing what the script does: "After generating requirements, run a coverage check. List all source files with ≥50 lines. Grep REQUIREMENTS.md for each class name. For any file with zero references that has behavioral contracts, generate requirements. Repeat until stable."

**Wrong: "not yet" on context window management.** That's actually the thing we should be thinking about *now*, because it affects how we write the SKILL.md instructions. The key instruction is "append to REQUIREMENTS.md after each subsystem, don't hold everything in memory and write once at the end." If we wait until after the loop converges to think about this, we'll have to rewrite Step 7 again. The instruction to write incrementally costs nothing to add now and prevents the agent from trying to generate 150 requirements in one shot and then writing them all at once.

The more interesting question Claude.ai raised is the stopping criterion refinement: after the first coverage pass fills obvious gaps, switch from "which files have no requirements" to "which requirements have no conditions of satisfaction that could generate a failing test." That's a quality check on top of the coverage check. We could add that as a second-stage termination criterion — first converge on file coverage, then do one final pass checking whether each requirement's conditions of satisfaction are actually testable. But that's a refinement for after we see what the loop produces.

How's the loop going? Has it gotten past iteration 1 yet?

## Assistant
*2026-04-03 22:34:03 UTC*

93 requirements. 73 of 81 source files covered. The 8 remaining uncovered files are all under 50 lines — exception wrappers, marker annotations, and internal plumbing with no behavioral contracts worth testing.

The gap-fill pass added 16 requirements across three new subsystems:

**Subsystem 8 (Date/time):** 5 requirements covering `DefaultDateTypeAdapter` locale/timezone capture, ISO-8601 parsing variants, SQL type optional-module support, `java.time` frozen wire shapes, and composite temporal adapter composition. This was the biggest gap Claude.ai identified — now covered.

**Subsystem 9 (Tree model bridge):** 7 requirements covering `JsonTreeReader` token/path fidelity, custom subclass rejection, nesting limit behavior, `JsonTreeWriter` structured building, `JsonArray.asList()` live view semantics, and `JsonPrimitive` construction/equality contracts.

**Subsystem 10 (Formatting/enums/types):** 4 requirements covering `FormattingStyle` immutability, enum adapter name/alias/toString precedence, and `GsonTypes` canonicalization and contextual resolution.

The model also made good skip decisions — it recognized that 17 of the 34 uncovered files were already covered by existing requirements under different names (e.g., `FieldNamingPolicy.java` is covered by REQ-012, `UnsafeAllocator.java` is covered by REQ-035/037, `ArrayTypeAdapter.java` is covered by REQ-040/093). That's the coverage check doing its job — it found the file was unreferenced, the model read it, and determined the behavioral contracts were already captured.

The progression: 48 → 70 → 77 → 93. Three passes, each finding genuinely different things. The loop converged in one iteration of gap-fill, which means the approach is efficient — you don't need 5 iterations, you need the coverage check to point the model at what it missed.

The quality looks strong too. REQ-081 (java.time frozen wire shape) is exactly the kind of requirement that catches real bugs — if someone "helpfully" changes the built-in `java.time` adapters to emit ISO strings instead of the object-shaped format, that breaks backward compatibility for every stored JSON document. That's an intent violation that structural review would never catch.

## Human
*2026-04-04 02:24:11 UTC*

claude.ai had this to say:

```
This is much better. 93 requirements, 73 of 81 files covered, and the gap-fill pass caught the biggest holes I flagged. Let me check what landed and what's still missing.

**Covered now that wasn't before:**

Date/time handling — REQ-078 through REQ-082 cover `DefaultDateTypeAdapter`, ISO-8601, SQL types, and `java.time`. These are solid, especially REQ-081 (frozen wire shape) which is exactly the kind of backward-compatibility requirement that catches real bugs.

Enum handling — REQ-091 covers name/alias/toString precedence. This was completely absent before. The conditions of satisfaction explicitly call out the precedence order, which is testable.

Tree model bridge — REQ-083 through REQ-089 cover `JsonTreeReader`, `JsonTreeWriter`, `JsonArray.asList()`, and `JsonPrimitive` semantics. REQ-084 (reject custom subclasses) and REQ-087 (live view rejects null) are specific enough to generate failing tests.

Type infrastructure — REQ-092 and REQ-093 cover `GsonTypes` canonicalization and contextual resolution. These are the internal machinery requirements that would catch type-resolution bugs.

**Still missing or thin:**

Primitive vs. wrapper null semantics during deserialization. What happens when JSON `null` hits an `int` field? REQ-027 (missing members preserve post-construction state) is about *absent* fields. The question of *explicit JSON null into a primitive field* is a different scenario — it should throw, not silently produce 0. This is a common production bug.

`deepCopy()` contract. REQ-052 and REQ-053 cover `JsonObject` mutation semantics, but there's nothing about `JsonElement.deepCopy()` preserving full nested structure independence — mutations to the copy must not affect the original.

HTML escaping specifics. REQ-003 mentions it as a default behavior bullet point. There's no standalone requirement about `disableHtmlEscaping()`, which characters get escaped by default (`<`, `>`, `&`, `=`, `'`), or the interaction with Unicode escaping. This is a real API surface with user expectations.

Built-in type adapter contracts for specific types. `BigDecimal` scale preservation, `URL` normalization behavior, `UUID` format, `AtomicInteger` thread-safety during serialization — each of these has implicit contracts that users depend on. The document covers the adapter *framework* well but not the specific *built-in adapters* beyond date/time and enum.

`JsonParser` entry points. REQ-017 covers trailing data detection for `Gson.fromJson()`, but `JsonParser.parseString()` and `JsonParser.parseReader()` are separate entry points with their own contracts. `JsonStreamParser` for reading multiple values from one stream has no coverage at all.

Field declaration order stability. Serialization output order matters for wire compatibility and snapshot testing. There's no requirement stating whether field order follows declaration order, inheritance order, or is undefined.

That said — 93 requirements with this level of detail is a genuinely useful specification document. The remaining gaps are real but they're the long tail. The coverage loop converging in two iterations with the mechanical check is the important finding: the approach works, scales to a real library, and produces requirements that could actually catch bugs. The gaps I'm listing would probably emerge from one more targeted pass focused on "built-in type adapter contracts" and "entry point contracts" as explicit categories.
```

what do you think?

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 02:24:33 UTC*

Claude.ai's analysis is accurate. The 93 requirements cover the architectural contracts and the subsystems that were completely missing (date/time, tree model, enums, type infrastructure). The remaining gaps are real but they're a different category — they're individual adapter behaviors and specific API entry points rather than whole missing subsystems.

The question is whether chasing those gaps is worth it for the playbook. I think some of them are, and some aren't:

**Worth adding to the SKILL.md as standard categories:** Primitive vs. wrapper null semantics (explicit JSON null into `int` field) is a common production bug and the kind of thing the playbook should systematically derive. `deepCopy()` independence is a tree-model invariant that any JSON library should guarantee. These are the "domain knowledge" requirements that the gap audit pass should catch — they're not Gson-specific, they're JSON-library-specific.

**Worth adding but Gson-specific:** HTML escaping specifics, `JsonParser` entry points, field ordering stability. These would come out of a more thorough Pass 1 derivation if the model didn't run out of steam at 70 requirements. The coverage loop approach handles this — if `JsonParser.java` had been in the uncovered list, the gap-fill would have written requirements for it. The issue is that the coverage check counts *any* reference as covered, so a file mentioned once in passing (like `JsonParser` getting 0 hits but `JsonStreamParser` getting 1 hit) might slip through.

**Probably not worth a separate pass:** Individual built-in type adapter contracts (BigDecimal scale, URL normalization, UUID format). These are real contracts but they're low-risk — mature, simple adapters that are heavily tested. The cost-benefit of writing 25 requirements for 25 adapters that each do one straightforward thing is poor compared to the structural requirements that catch cross-cutting bugs.

The more interesting takeaway is Claude.ai's point about the coverage check: counting class name references is a coarse signal. `JsonParser.java` has zero references but `JsonStreamParser` has one (because it appears in a "covered" file). A file that's mentioned once in a condition of satisfaction isn't the same as a file with dedicated requirements. A refinement would be to count requirements that *primarily reference* a file (in the References field) rather than just grep for the class name anywhere. But that's an optimization — the current approach got us from 48 to 93 and caught the major gaps.

For now I'd say: the 93-requirement version is good enough to rerun the code review against. The gaps Claude.ai identified are real but marginal — they'd add maybe 10-15 more requirements, and the probability of any one of them catching a bug that the existing 93 miss is low. The bigger win is running the three-pass review against these 93 requirements and seeing what it finds that the 48-requirement review missed.

Want to do that next, or do you want to chase the remaining gaps first?

## Human
*2026-04-04 02:26:02 UTC*

---

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 02:26:18 UTC*

Ambiguous or missing requirements. It's not even close.

The data on this goes back decades — Boehm's cost escalation curve, the Standish CHAOS reports, IBM's Systems Sciences Institute studies. A defect introduced in requirements costs 50-200x more to fix in production than one caught during requirements review. Not because the fix is technically harder, but because the wrong requirement propagates through design, implementation, and testing. Every downstream artifact is built on the wrong assumption.

The Gson bug is a perfect example. The duplicate-key check wasn't a coding error — whoever wrote `if (replaced != null)` understood `Map.put()` semantics. The defect was a missing requirement: nobody wrote down "duplicate keys must be detected even when the first value is null." The code was correct for the requirement that existed in the developer's head (detect duplicates). It was wrong for the requirement that should have existed (detect duplicates in all cases, including null values). That gap persisted for years in a library with 23K+ stars and extensive test coverage.

And that's exactly what we're building here. The playbook's value proposition is that it closes the gap between "what the code does" and "what the code should do." The 93 requirements are the specification of "should." Every requirement we miss is a class of bugs we can't catch. Every requirement we get wrong is a false positive that erodes trust in the system.

So the question is: are 93 requirements the right level of coverage for Gson, or are we leaving defect classes on the table? Claude.ai identified concrete gaps — primitive null semantics, `deepCopy()` independence, HTML escaping, entry point contracts, field ordering. Each of those is a category where a real bug could exist and our review wouldn't catch it because there's no requirement to check against.

What are you thinking?

*2026-04-04 02:26:47 UTC*

## Human
*2026-04-04 02:26:47 UTC*

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 02:27:26 UTC*

The problem is that every approach so far asks the model to do two things at once: figure out what contracts exist AND write requirements for them. It conflates discovery with documentation. The model runs out of attention because it's trying to hold the entire behavioral surface in its head while also producing formatted requirements.

Split them apart.

**Step 1: Contract extraction (per-file).** For each source file, the model reads it and outputs a raw list of behavioral contracts. Not requirements — just observations. "This file validates X. This method promises Y. This error path assumes Z. This null check exists because W." Short lines, no template, no user stories. Pure discovery. Write them to a working file: `quality/contracts.md`.

**Step 2: Requirements derivation (from contracts).** Read the contracts file and the documentation. Group related contracts. Write requirements in the full template. The model isn't discovering anymore — it's just formatting and enriching what it already found.

**Step 3: Contract coverage verification.** For each contract in `contracts.md`, check whether a requirement covers it. Mark each contract as COVERED or UNCOVERED. This is the loop check — if there are uncovered contracts, generate requirements for them.

**Step 4: Completeness assertion.** The model reads the final REQUIREMENTS.md, the contracts file, and the source tree. It writes a verdict: either "complete" with a brief justification, or "incomplete" with a list of remaining gaps. If incomplete, go back to Step 1 for the specific files with gaps.

The key insight is that `contracts.md` is the external memory. It makes the model's understanding of the behavioral surface explicit and checkable. Right now the model implicitly decides what contracts exist and what to write requirements for, and it drops things on the floor because it forgets. With a contracts file, forgetting is visible — you can grep for uncovered contracts.

Let me build it.

## Human
*2026-04-04 02:29:29 UTC*

a little back and forth with claude.ai that may help steer this a bit:

```
The four-step split is the right architectural insight. Conflating discovery with documentation is exactly why the model produces 70 requirements and stops — it's doing the hard cognitive work of identifying contracts simultaneously with the formatting work of writing user stories and conditions of satisfaction. Separating them means the discovery pass can be fast and exhaustive (short lines, no template overhead), and the documentation pass can be thorough without also having to think about what to look for.
The contracts.md file as external memory is the key idea. Right now when the model "forgets" about HTML escaping or deepCopy(), that forgetting is invisible — there's no record that it ever noticed those contracts. With a contracts file, every behavioral observation is written down before any requirements work begins. If a contract exists in contracts.md but has no corresponding requirement, that's a visible, greppable gap. The coverage check becomes precise instead of approximate.
The thing I'd watch for: Step 1 (per-file contract extraction) could produce a lot of noise. A mature file like Gson.java might yield 50 raw contract observations, many of which are trivial ("this method returns a String") or redundant with each other. Step 2 needs to be good at grouping and deduplicating, not just formatting. The instruction should say something like "group contracts that describe the same behavioral boundary, even if they appear in different files." Otherwise you get 300 raw contracts that produce 200 requirements, most of which are just different angles on the same 80 behaviors.
The other risk is that this process is expensive. Four steps, each potentially iterating, each reading substantial amounts of code. For a library the size of Gson, Step 1 alone (read every source file, extract contracts) could consume significant tokens. The per-file approach helps — each file is a bounded unit of work — but 81 files times even a modest extraction pass adds up. The question is whether the quality improvement over the current 93-requirement approach justifies the cost. For the playbook's reference implementation and for the article, yes — getting this right is the whole point. For everyday use on a random project, you might want a "standard" mode (current approach, ~93 requirements in 3 passes) and a "thorough" mode (four-step extraction, ~150 requirements in more passes).
The "most expensive root cause" exchange is the right framing for the article. The playbook exists because missing requirements are the most expensive class of defect, and AI makes comprehensive requirements derivation cheap enough to actually do. The progression from 48 → 70 → 77 → 93 → whatever this produces is the proof that the approach converges on genuine coverage. Each step found different things because each step used a different discovery mechanism. That's the story.re: expense -- did you catch the part where copilot charged me $0.04 for 1.5m tokens?11:35 PMRecalibrated cost concerns upon recognizing negligible operational expensesRecalibrated cost concerns upon recognizing negligible operational expensesRight, that completely kills my cost concern. At $0.04 per 1.5M tokens, the entire four-step extraction for Gson — read every file, extract contracts, write requirements, verify coverage — costs maybe $0.20 total. There's no "standard" vs. "thorough" mode tradeoff to make. Just run the thorough version every time. The cost of missing a requirement that would have caught a bug is infinitely higher than twenty cents.
This also changes the calculus for the playbook's design. Every place where I've been thinking "is this extra pass worth it?" — the answer is yes, always, because the marginal cost of one more Copilot CLI call is effectively zero. The gap audit pass, the cross-requirement consistency check, the contract extraction step, the completeness assertion — run all of them. The constraint isn't cost, it's time (waiting for the passes to complete) and quality of the instructions (making sure each pass does its job well).
That's actually a point worth making in the article. The reason quality engineering was abandoned wasn't that the techniques didn't work — it was that they required expensive humans. At $0.04 per pass, the entire comprehensive requirements derivation for a 14,000-line library costs less than a cup of coffee. The "too expensive" argument is dead.
```

Before I update the SKILL.md, let me make sure the pipeline is ready to run. Here's what you've got:

```
control_prompts/
  prompt_step1_extract_contracts.md    ← Read all source files, list every behavioral contract
  prompt_step2_derive_requirements.md  ← Group contracts, enrich with intent, write requirements
  prompt_step3_verify_coverage.md      ← Cross-reference contracts ↔ requirements, fix gaps
  prompt_step4_completeness_check.md   ← Domain checklist, testability audit, consistency check
  run_requirements_v2.sh              ← Orchestrates all four steps with iteration on Step 3
```

The loop structure: Steps 1-2 run once. Step 3 can loop up to 3 times if it keeps finding gaps. Step 4 runs once as the final gate. You can restart from any step with `bash run_requirements_v2.sh 3` to skip to Step 3.

Before running this, you'll want a clean starting point — either delete the current `quality/REQUIREMENTS.md` or rename it. The pipeline generates everything fresh from source files:

   The overarching goal evolved through the session: starting with "improve the template and remove the count cap," progressing to "make it cover all subsystems," then to "make it loop until complete," and finally to "build a rigorous 4-step pipeline that separates contract discovery from requirement derivation, with mechanical verification and a completeness gate." The user explicitly stated: "Let's keep working on this, because it's arguably the most important part of the entire system. All of quality engineering depends on good requirements" and "I'm thinking we f'ing get this right."

2. Key Technical Concepts:

   - **Requirements progression**: 48 (original) → 70 (template restructure) → 77 (domain gap audit) → 93 (file coverage loop) → TBD (v2 pipeline)
   - **User stories with "so that" clauses**: From Learning Agile — forces articulation of intent behind requirements
   - **Conditions of satisfaction**: From Learning Agile — testable acceptance criteria that turn vague requirements into checklists
   - **Implementation notes** (renamed from "Rationale"): Technical mechanism explanation, distinct from user-facing motivation
   - **Alternative paths**: From use case templates in Applied Software Project Management — multiple code paths that must all satisfy a requirement
   - **Contract extraction**: Separating discovery (what behavioral contracts exist) from documentation (writing formal requirements)
   - **Mechanical termination criteria**: Using deterministic checks (file coverage grep, contract cross-reference) instead of model judgment for loop termination
   - **File-based external memory**: Writing contracts.md, coverage_matrix.md as working files so the model doesn't need to hold everything in context
   - **Boehm's cost escalation curve**: Requirements defects cost 50-200x more to fix in production
   - **The 65%/35% split**: Structural code review catches ~65% of defects; the remaining ~35% are intent violations requiring requirements
   - **Two-pass derivation experiment** (from NSQ): Proved that cross-requirement consistency checking is essential (found 2 of 3 target defects)
   - **Pipeline model**: GPT-5.4 via `gh copilot` CLI with `--yolo` flag

4. Errors and Fixes:
   - **`run_requirements_loop.sh` exit code bug**: The script had `set -euo pipefail` which caused bash to abort when `check_coverage.py` returned exit code 1 (its signal for "uncovered files exist, keep going"). The `PIPESTATUS` capture on the next line never executed. Fixed by wrapping the coverage check call with `set +e` before and `set -e` after. Also fixed the same issue for the final coverage report at the end of the script. User reported: "it ran and exited immediately" with full output showing the coverage check ran but nothing else happened.

   - **Bash grep mangling**: First attempt at file coverage analysis used a bash one-liner with `while read` and grep in subshells, which produced garbled output due to newlines in grep output interfering with the integer comparison `[ "$hits" -eq 0 ]`. Rewrote as a Python script (`check_coverage.py`) which handled the comparison cleanly.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Why only 48 requirements**: Traced to explicit "Expect 30-60 requirements" instruction in the control prompt (line 39). The model hit the expected range and stopped. Not a system limit or early stop — the ceiling was in the prompt.
   
   - **Coverage gaps at 77 requirements**: Mechanical file coverage check revealed 34 of 81 source files had zero requirements referencing them, totaling 5,720 lines of uncovered behavioral surface. Biggest gaps: date/time adapters, tree model bridge, enum handling, array/collection adapters, parsing entry points.
   
   - **Model attention/fatigue problem**: The core insight that drove the v2 pipeline design: the model "runs out of steam" after writing ~70 requirements because it's simultaneously discovering contracts and writing formal requirements. Solution: separate discovery (contract extraction) from documentation (requirement derivation), using files as external memory.
   
   - **Loop termination**: Evolved from coarse (file name grep) to fine-grained (per-contract cross-reference). The v2 pipeline uses contracts.md as the ground truth — every contract must map to a requirement condition of satisfaction.

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 05:11:44 UTC*

This is a significant step forward. Here's the breakdown:

**Pipeline execution summary (55 minutes total):**

| Step | Duration | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Step 1: Contract extraction | ~13 min | 826 contracts from 81 files |
| Step 2: Derive requirements | ~22 min | 90 requirements |
| Step 3: Coverage verification | ~12 min (2 iterations) | 4 gaps filled → 94 → verified at 100% |
| Step 4: Completeness check | ~7 min | 5 more requirements → 99, verdict: COMPLETE |

**What went right:**

The contract-extraction approach did exactly what we designed it to do. 826 contracts across all 9 categories (411 method, 134 error, 59 null, 58 config, 53 invariant, 49 compat, 33 lifecycle, 20 thread, 9 ordering). That's an order of magnitude more granular than what the model was discovering when it tried to simultaneously find contracts and write requirements.

The coverage matrix hit 100% after one fix-up pass — 824 of 831 items covered on first draft, 7 gaps patched. That's the mechanical cross-reference doing its job; the model can't skip contracts when there's a written checklist to cross off.

The completeness report shows all 17 domains covered, no testability issues, no consistency conflicts. The domains that were persistently missing in the v3 version — primitive vs wrapper null, deepCopy, HTML escaping, built-in type adapters, JsonParser entry points, field ordering — all have explicit REQ citations now.

**The numbers tell the story:**

- v1 (template only): 48 requirements, 337 lines
- v2 (template + gap audit): 77 requirements, 781 lines  
- v3 (iterative gap fill): 93 requirements, ~950 lines
- **v4 (contract-first pipeline): 99 requirements, 971 lines, 23 categories**

And it's not just more requirements — the requirement quality is higher. Look at REQ-094 (SQL Timestamp precision): it identifies the nanosecond truncation behavior, explains the delegation chain to the Date adapter, gives a concrete condition of satisfaction ("nanoseconds finer than milliseconds are silently discarded"), and tells callers what to do about it. The v3 version's date requirements didn't get anywhere near that specificity.

**What I'd want to verify before baking this into SKILL.md:**

The coverage matrix is thorough but the mapping in the matrix is oddly coarse for some files — look at how every Gson.java contract maps to the same long list of REQ numbers. That's the model being lazy about individual mappings; it's saying "these contracts are covered *somewhere* in these 11 requirements" rather than pinpointing which condition of satisfaction covers which contract. The coverage is likely real, but the traceability could be tighter.

The other question is reproducibility. This ran on GPT-5.4 with one specific prompt sequence. We'd want to know whether the 4-step structure produces consistently better results than the old single-pass, not just on this one run. But as a first result, 826 contracts → 99 requirements with 100% coverage and a COMPLETE verdict is exactly what we were aiming for.

The pipeline design is validated. Ready to talk about how to fold this into the SKILL.md?

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 05:13:07 UTC*

You're putting your finger on something important. Right now REQUIREMENTS.md reads like a legal contract — every clause is precise and testable, but there's no narrative thread. A developer reading it would know what every requirement *says* but not what the software *is*. There's no connective tissue explaining why the builder pattern exists, why there's both a tree model and a streaming API, why adapter resolution is ordered the way it is, or what the overall data flow looks like from `Gson.fromJson()` through adapter lookup through the reader stack.

What's missing is what you'd find in the opening pages of a good technical spec — the part where the author says "here's how this system works and why it's built this way" before diving into the formal requirements.

I'd add a Step 5: an **architecture narrative pass** that reads the finished REQUIREMENTS.md and adds three things:

**First**, a top-level architecture overview. Two or three paragraphs explaining what Gson is, how data flows through it, and what the major subsystems are. The kind of thing you'd put on a whiteboard when onboarding someone.

**Second**, category-level narratives — a short prose introduction before each category section that explains the design intent and how this subsystem relates to the others. Right now "Category: adapter protocol and composition" jumps straight into REQ-020. Instead, it should open with a paragraph explaining what the adapter protocol is, why Gson uses a factory chain rather than a type registry, and what "composition" means in this context. That paragraph is what turns a list of requirements into an explanation.

**Third**, a cross-cutting concerns section that documents the architectural philosophies that span multiple categories: the threading model (immutable instances, stateless adapters), the error philosophy (fail-fast with diagnostic context), the backward compatibility strategy (LEGACY_STRICT as a compatibility bridge), the null contract (what null means at each layer). These explain *why* many of the individual requirements exist.

The prompt for this step would read the contracts file and the requirements file and produce the narrative layer. The model has all the raw material — it extracted 826 contracts, it grouped them into 23 categories, it knows the code. It just hasn't been asked to *explain* yet, only to *specify*.

The key instruction would be something like: "A new engineer is reading this document to understand how Gson works before modifying it. Write the narrative sections they need so that every requirement makes sense in context. Do not change or remove any existing requirement — add the explanatory tissue around them."

Want me to write that prompt and add it as Step 5 in the pipeline?

---

All done. Here's what I built:

**New file: `prompt_step5_narrative_pass.md`** — the final step that transforms the specification into a guide. It instructs the model to:

1. **Project Overview** at the top — what Gson is, who uses it, how data flows through it, and the design philosophy. Written as connected prose, not bullet lists.

2. **Use Cases** — 6-8 structured use cases in the style from Applied Software Project Management: actor, preconditions, numbered steps, postconditions, alternative paths, and explicit REQ-NNN traceability. Covers the major patterns from basic serialization through thread-safe shared instances.

3. **Cross-Cutting Concerns** — prose sections on threading, null contract, error philosophy, backward compatibility, and configuration composition. Each references specific requirements.

4. **Category Narratives** — 2-4 sentence introductions before each category explaining what it covers, how it connects to other categories, and what a reviewer should watch for.

5. **Top-Down Reorder** — restructures categories from user-facing (Core API) down to infrastructure (backward compatibility), and folds the catch-all "Coverage Additions" into proper categories.

6. **Sequential Renumbering** — REQ-001 through REQ-NNN following document order after the reorder.

The prompt has a hard rule: no requirement content may be deleted, merged, or weakened. It's purely additive narrative.

**Updated files:**
- `run_requirements_v2.sh` — now a 5-step pipeline with file-rename normalization (lowercase → UPPER_CASE) after Steps 1 and 4, plus a sanity check that Step 5 doesn't drop requirements
- All four existing prompts (Steps 1-4) — updated to reference `CONTRACTS.md`, `COVERAGE_MATRIX.md`, `COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md`

To run just the narrative pass against the existing pipeline output: `bash control_prompts/run_requirements_v2.sh 5`

*2026-04-04 12:07:37 UTC*

This landed really well. Here's my assessment:

**What the narrative pass achieved:**

The document went from 971 lines to 1,195 — that's 224 lines of pure explanatory tissue added without losing a single requirement. The "Coverage Additions" catch-all section is gone; those requirements (REQ-091 through REQ-094 from the previous run) got folded into their proper categories.

**The Project Overview** (lines 6-14) is exactly the kind of thing you described — it answers who uses Gson, what they use it for, how data flows through the system, and why the architecture looks the way it does. Written as connected prose, not bullets. A new team member could read these four paragraphs and understand the software before seeing a single requirement.

**The 8 Use Cases** follow the Applied Software Project Management structure: actor, preconditions, numbered steps, postconditions, alternative paths, and explicit REQ cross-references. They cover the full range from "basic serialize/deserialize with defaults" through "reuse one configured Gson across threads." The traceability is there — you can go from Use Case 4 (generic types with TypeToken) straight to REQ-004, REQ-048-052.

**Cross-Cutting Concerns** captures the architectural invariants in prose: threading model, null contract, error philosophy, backward compatibility, configuration composition. Each one cites the specific requirements it spans.

**Category narratives** — every `### Category:` section now opens with 2-4 sentences of context before the first requirement. Compare "Category: configuration surface" before (jumped straight into REQ-002) versus now ("This category covers the choices callers make before any JSON is read or written...").

**The reorder** flows top-down: Core API → Type Adapter System → Reflection Layer → Streaming Layer → Type System → Null Handling → Collections → Numerics/Dates → Field Management → Annotations → HTML/Output → Error Handling → Thread Safety → Backward Compatibility. That's a natural reading order from "what a user touches first" down to "infrastructure concerns."

The requirement count held at exactly 99 — the sanity check in the script confirmed it. No drops, no phantom additions.

This is ready to use. You could hand this document to a team and say "implement this" and they'd have a starting point. Want to update EXPERIMENT.md with the Step 5 results, or are you thinking about folding the whole 5-step pipeline into SKILL.md next?

## Human
*2026-04-04 12:21:18 UTC*

Yes, this is a massive improvement, and we're almost there. Before we seal this up, here's what Claude.ai said – let me know what you think:

```
This is a substantial improvement. Let me assess both lenses.

**Readability: This reads like a real specification now.**

The project overview is the best part of the document. Someone who's never used Gson could read those four paragraphs and understand what the library does, who uses it, how data flows through it, and why it's designed the way it is. That's exactly what use cases need as context.

The 10 use cases are well-structured and cover the right scenarios — POJO defaults, custom adapters, builder configuration, generic types, streaming, tree model, legacy JSON, threading, platform restrictions, and date/time. The "Requirements" line at the end of each use case creates traceability from scenario to specification. A new team member reading this would understand both *what Gson promises* and *where to look for the detailed rules*.

The cross-cutting concerns section (threading model, null contract, error philosophy, backward compatibility, configuration composition) is a smart addition. These are the things that span the entire document and that reviewers need to hold in their head while reading any individual requirement. Putting them upfront as a conceptual map is what a good quality engineer would do.

The category introductions before each requirement group are doing real work. "If these token and state-machine rules drift, seemingly unrelated higher-level APIs start failing in subtle and hard-to-diagnose ways" — that's not filler, that's telling the reviewer *why this section matters*.

**Completeness: Major gaps closed, a few remaining.**

REQ-056 (primitive vs. wrapper null semantics) is the single most important addition. This was my number one gap item across every previous version. The conditions of satisfaction correctly distinguish "primitive field keeps its existing value" from "wrapper field becomes null" and cover the custom-adapter-returns-null case. This requirement alone would catch a class of production bugs.

REQ-068 (arrays and collections) closes another significant gap — ordering, encounter-order semantics, and element null propagation are now specified.

REQ-069 (enum value adapters) with name/alias/toString precedence, null-on-unknown, and obfuscation resilience is thorough and testable.

The date/time, tree bridge, streaming lifecycle, and SQL timestamp precision sections are all solid from previous versions and well-integrated here.

**What's still missing:**

`deepCopy()` independence — still absent across all iterations. `JsonElement.deepCopy()` must produce a structurally independent copy where mutations to the copy don't affect the original and vice versa. This is a tree-model invariant that matters for any code that clones a `JsonElement` before modifying it.

`JsonParser` standalone entry points — `JsonParser.parseString()` and `JsonParser.parseReader()` are distinct from `Gson.fromJson()` and have their own contracts about what they return (always a `JsonElement`, no type binding) and how they handle errors. `JsonStreamParser` for reading multiple JSON values from a single stream has no coverage at all.

HTML escaping specifics — the section covers the non-executable prefix, prefix tolerance, pretty printing, and `FormattingStyle`, but there's no requirement about the actual HTML escaping behavior: which characters get escaped by default (`<`, `>`, `&`, `=`, `'`), what `disableHtmlEscaping()` changes, and how HTML escaping interacts with Unicode output. This is a real API surface that users configure.

Built-in type adapter contracts for specific types — `BigDecimal` scale preservation, `URL`/`URI` normalization, `UUID` format, `AtomicInteger`/`AtomicLong`/`AtomicBoolean` behavior, `Calendar` serialization format. Each has implicit contracts users depend on. The document covers the adapter *framework* thoroughly but not what specific built-in adapters promise.

Field serialization ordering — whether output field order follows declaration order, inheritance order, or is undefined. This matters for wire compatibility and snapshot testing.

`toJsonTree`/`fromJsonTree` — these are distinct entry points from `toJson`/`fromJson` that produce or consume `JsonElement` trees instead of strings. They have their own edge cases around null handling and type resolution.

That said, the remaining gaps are genuinely the long tail. This document has gone from 48 terse code contracts to 99 detailed requirements with use cases, cross-cutting concerns, category narratives, and the full template — and it's readable by a human who doesn't know Gson's internals. That's a specification document, not a code review artifact. The progression from where this started to where it is now is the proof that the playbook's requirements derivation pipeline works.
```

---

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 12:21:33 UTC*

Claude.ai's assessment is accurate. The narrative pass landed well — no argument there. The remaining gaps are real, and they fall into two distinct categories that matter for how we fix them.

The first category is **entry points and API surface the pipeline should have found but didn't**: `deepCopy()`, `JsonParser.parseString()`/`parseReader()`, `JsonStreamParser`, `toJsonTree`/`fromJsonTree` as distinct paths, HTML escaping character set, field serialization ordering. These aren't obscure — they're public API with real contracts. The contract extraction (Step 1) probably *did* find some of these as individual contracts in the 826, but Step 2's grouping phase either absorbed them into broader requirements without giving them explicit conditions of satisfaction, or the completeness check in Step 4 didn't flag them because the domain checklist was too coarse.

The second category is **built-in adapter specifics**: `BigDecimal` scale, `URL`/`URI` normalization, `UUID` format, `AtomicInteger`/`AtomicLong`, `Calendar`. These are the long tail — each is a real contract users depend on, but the pipeline treated the adapter *framework* as the requirement boundary and didn't drill into what individual adapters promise.

The fix isn't another one-off pass. It's sharpening Step 4's domain checklist so the pipeline catches these automatically on future runs. Right now the checklist says things like "Tree model: mutation semantics, deep copy, null normalization" — but it doesn't call out `deepCopy()` independence as a specific item. It says "Entry points" but lists only `Gson.fromJson`, `Gson.toJson`, etc. — no `JsonParser`, no `JsonStreamParser`, no `toJsonTree`/`fromJsonTree`. And it has no line item for built-in adapter contracts at all.

If we tighten the checklist, the pipeline becomes self-correcting. Want me to update the Step 4 prompt with the sharper checklist and then you can re-run steps 4 and 5 to close the gaps?

---

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 12:22:46 UTC*

Here's the current Step 4 domain checklist — these are the items the completeness check is required to verify:

- Null handling: explicit null, absent fields, null keys, null values in collections
- Type coercion: string↔number, string↔boolean, number precision, overflow
- Primitive vs wrapper: int vs Integer null semantics during deserialization
- Generic types: erasure boundaries, wildcard handling, recursive generics
- Thread safety: concurrent access, publication safety, adapter cache visibility
- Error diagnostics: exception types, path context, location information
- Resource management: stream closing, reader/writer lifecycle
- Backward compatibility: wire format stability, API behavioral stability
- Security: DoS protection (nesting depth, string length), injection prevention
- Encoding: Unicode, BOM, surrogate pairs, escape sequences
- Date/time: format precedence, timezone handling, precision
- Collections: arrays, lists, sets, maps, queues — empty, null elements, ordering
- Enums: name resolution, aliases, unknown values
- Polymorphism: runtime type vs declared type, adapter delegation
- Tree model: mutation semantics, deep copy, null normalization
- Configuration: builder immutability, instance isolation, option composition
- Entry points: Gson.fromJson, Gson.toJson, Gson.toJsonTree, Gson.fromJsonTree, JsonParser.parseString, JsonStreamParser

That's 17 items. Based on Claude.ai's gap analysis, I'd propose adding or sharpening these:

- **Tree model** → add: `deepCopy()` structural independence (mutations to copy don't affect original)
- **Entry points** → expand: `toJsonTree`/`fromJsonTree` as distinct paths with own null/type contracts; `JsonParser.parseString()`/`parseReader()` returning `JsonElement` not bound types; `JsonStreamParser` for multi-value streams
- **HTML escaping** → new item: which characters are escaped by default (`<`, `>`, `&`, `=`, `'`), what `disableHtmlEscaping()` changes, interaction with Unicode output
- **Built-in type adapter contracts** → new item: `BigDecimal` scale preservation, `URL`/`URI` normalization, `UUID` format, `AtomicInteger`/`AtomicLong`/`AtomicBoolean`, `Calendar` serialization format
- **Field serialization ordering** → new item: whether output field order follows declaration order, inheritance order, or is undefined; implications for wire compatibility and snapshot testing

That would bring it to 20 items, with the three weakest spots (entry points, tree model, HTML escaping) sharpened and two new categories (built-in adapter contracts, field ordering) added.

---

## Human
*2026-04-04 12:24:54 UTC*

Here's what Claude.ai had to say. It flagged my biggest concern – that the checklist is very GSON specific. I expect that as we move on to the next set of repos, the main changes we'll make will be to expand this domain checklist.

```
The approach is right — sharpening the checklist so the pipeline catches these automatically is better than doing another one-off pass. The whole point of the playbook is that it produces consistent results without someone who knows the codebase looking over its shoulder. If the checklist is good enough, the pipeline finds deepCopy() and JsonParser on any JSON library, not just Gson.

The 20-item checklist looks solid. A few reactions:

The built-in type adapter contracts item needs a structural instruction, not just a list of types. "BigDecimal scale, URL normalization, UUID format" is Gson-specific. The checklist item should say something like "for each built-in type adapter that handles a JDK or platform type, state what the adapter promises about format, precision, normalization, and round-trip fidelity." That generalizes to any library that ships built-in handlers for standard types — Jackson has the same issue, so does Moshi, so does any serialization framework.

Field serialization ordering is the right addition but it's actually harder than it sounds. Gson's field order is declaration order within a class, parent fields before child fields, and that's an implementation detail that's become a de facto contract. The requirement should state whether this ordering is *promised* or merely *observed* — because if it's not promised, a reviewer shouldn't flag a change to it as a bug, even if it breaks snapshot tests. The checklist should prompt the pipeline to determine which category the ordering falls into.

The HTML escaping item should also cover `JsonWriter.setHtmlSafe()` as the writer-level control distinct from the builder-level `disableHtmlEscaping()`. Those are two different API surfaces that affect the same behavior, and the requirement should specify how they interact — does the writer-level setting override the builder-level setting, or vice versa?

One thing missing from the checklist that I'd add: **`toString()` and `hashCode()`/`equals()` contracts for public types.** `JsonElement.toString()` produces JSON text. `JsonPrimitive.equals()` has specific numeric equivalence rules (already covered in REQ-075). But `JsonObject.equals()`, `JsonArray.equals()`, and `JsonElement.hashCode()` also have behavioral contracts that users depend on for comparison and map-key usage. These are the kind of contracts that nobody thinks to write down until someone changes the implementation and breaks downstream code.

Otherwise, the 20-item checklist at the right level of specificity would have caught every gap I flagged. That's the test — if the checklist had existed from the start, would the pipeline have produced the missing requirements? For deepCopy(), yes — the tree model item would have prompted it. For JsonParser, yes — the expanded entry points item covers it. For HTML escaping, yes — the new item is explicit enough. For built-in adapters, yes if the instruction is structural rather than a list of type names. For field ordering, yes with the clarification about promised vs. observed.

Tell Cowork to update Step 4 and rerun. The gaps should close.
```

---

But `JsonObject.equals()`, `JsonArray.equals()`, and `JsonElement.hashCode()` also have behavioral contracts that users depend on for comparison and map-key usage. These are the kind of contracts that nobody thinks to write down until someone changes the implementation and breaks downstream code.

Otherwise, the 20-item checklist at the right level of specificity would have caught every gap I flagged. That's the test — if the checklist had existed from the start, would the pipeline have produced the missing requirements? For deepCopy(), yes — the tree model item would have prompted it. For JsonParser, yes — the expanded entry points item covers it. For HTML escaping, yes — the new item is explicit enough. For built-in adapters, yes if the instruction is structural rather than a list of type names. For field ordering, yes with the clarification about promised vs. observed.

Tell Cowork to update Step 4 and rerun. The gaps should close.
```

Done. The checklist went from 17 items to 22, and more importantly, the items that were too coarse got sharpened:

**Sharpened items:**
- **Tree model** now explicitly requires `deepCopy()` structural independence
- **Entry points** is now a structural instruction ("if the library has N distinct ways to start a read or write, there must be N sets of contracts") rather than a list of method names — this generalizes to Jackson, Moshi, or anything else

**New items:**
- **HTML and output escaping** — specific characters, builder-vs-writer interaction, Unicode
- **Built-in type adapter contracts** — framed as "for each built-in adapter, state what it promises about format, precision, normalization, and round-trip fidelity" rather than a Gson-specific type list
- **Field serialization ordering** — with the promised-vs-observed distinction so the pipeline determines which category it falls into
- **Identity contracts** — `toString()`, `hashCode()`, `equals()` on public types

Every item is written to generalize. When you run this against a different repo, the pipeline will ask "does this library have built-in type handlers? what do they promise?" rather than "does it have BigDecimal scale preservation?" — the specific types come from the contract extraction, the checklist just ensures the completeness check looks for them.

To close the Gson gaps: `bash control_prompts/run_requirements_v2.sh 4`

## Human
*2026-04-04 12:32:33 UTC*

if we're happy with the results of this run for gson, the next step is to take all of this and turn it into an updated SKILL.md that does the same kind of iteration as the script. what are the mechanics of how that iteration works?

---

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 12:33:01 UTC*

The current pipeline runs 5 separate `gh copilot` invocations orchestrated by a shell script. Each invocation gets a fresh context window, reads specific files, and produces an output file that the next invocation reads. The shell script handles sequencing, loop control, and sanity checks.

In SKILL.md, all of that has to happen inside a single agent session. No external orchestrator. The question is what actually makes the pipeline work and which parts translate.

**What the shell script actually does:**

The script's real job is three things: sequencing, file-based memory handoff, and loop termination. Steps 1→2→3→4→5 must run in order. Each step reads the previous step's output file rather than relying on what the model remembers. And Step 3 loops up to 3 times based on a mechanical check (did the requirement count change?).

**What translates directly:**

File-based external memory is the core mechanism, and it works the same way in a single session. The agent writes `quality/CONTRACTS.md`, then reads it back to derive requirements. Writing to a file and reading it back forces the agent to work from the written artifact rather than from a fading memory of what it discovered. That's why we separated contract extraction from requirement derivation in the first place — the model runs out of attention when doing both simultaneously. The file boundary is what resets focus.

The sequential structure also translates. SKILL.md can say "Phase A: extract contracts and write CONTRACTS.md. Phase B: read CONTRACTS.md and derive requirements into REQUIREMENTS.md. Phase C: cross-reference and fix gaps." The agent does them in order within one session.

**What's harder in a single session:**

The loop. The shell script runs Step 3 up to 3 times by invoking a fresh `gh copilot` session each iteration. Each iteration gets a clean context window. In a single agent session, the agent accumulates context as it goes — by iteration 3 of the coverage loop, it's carrying the memory of iterations 1 and 2. That's more context pressure, but it's manageable if the instruction is concrete: "Count uncovered contracts in COVERAGE_MATRIX.md. If the count is zero, move on. If not, update REQUIREMENTS.md, regenerate the matrix, and check again. Maximum 3 iterations."

The other constraint is context window size. The 5-step pipeline was partly designed to manage this — each `gh copilot` invocation only reads the files it needs, not everything. In a single session, the agent reads contracts (1,010 lines), then requirements (971+ lines), then coverage (873 lines), and so on. For a project the size of Gson that's fine. For a larger codebase, the contract file alone could get large. The SKILL.md instructions should tell the agent to work from the files on disk rather than trying to hold everything in memory — read a section, process it, write output, move to the next section.

**What the SKILL.md Step 7 would look like structurally:**

```
Step 7: Requirements generation (5 phases)

Phase A — Contract extraction
  Read all source files. Write quality/CONTRACTS.md.
  [contract format and category instructions]

Phase B — Requirement derivation  
  Read quality/CONTRACTS.md and documentation. Write quality/REQUIREMENTS.md.
  [grouping, enrichment, template instructions]

Phase C — Coverage verification (loop, max 3 iterations)
  Read CONTRACTS.md + REQUIREMENTS.md. Cross-reference.
  Write quality/COVERAGE_MATRIX.md. Fix gaps. 
  If uncovered count > 0, repeat. If 0, proceed.

Phase D — Completeness check
  Read everything. Apply 22-item domain checklist.
  Write quality/COMPLETENESS_REPORT.md. Fix gaps.

Phase E — Narrative pass
  Save REQUIREMENTS_pre_narrative.md.
  Add project overview, use cases, cross-cutting concerns,
  category narratives. Reorder top-down. Renumber.
```

Each phase explicitly names the files it reads and the files it writes. That's the discipline that makes it work without an orchestrator — the file names are the contract between phases.

The main decision is whether the 22-item domain checklist lives in SKILL.md (making it generic) or in a separate file that SKILL.md references (allowing per-project customization). Given your point that the checklist will expand as you hit new repos, a separate file might be cleaner — SKILL.md defines the process, a domain checklist file defines what "complete" means for the project's domain.

## Human
*2026-04-04 12:35:39 UTC*

That looks good, but because it's AI it's not 100% reliable. We should give the user a fallback where they can tell review requirements and if they think it's missing something they can rerun the requirements generation to refine the requirements by doing another pass through step 7 and updating REQUIREMENTS.md (and backing it up each time so they can compare what changed). They can also provide some hints about things that were missed. What do you think about that -- is that the right way to handle it?

---

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 12:36:00 UTC*

Yes, that's exactly right. The pipeline gets you most of the way there, but the person reviewing the output knows things the model doesn't — domain expertise, edge cases they've been burned by, contracts that matter to their specific use case. Giving them a structured way to feed that back in is the correct design.

The mechanism I'd propose is simple. After the pipeline finishes, the user reviews REQUIREMENTS.md. If they spot gaps, they write their feedback into a file — something like `quality/REFINEMENT_HINTS.md` — in plain language. Things like "you missed deepCopy() independence on JsonElement" or "the built-in adapters for BigDecimal and UUID need explicit precision/format contracts" or "field ordering is observed behavior in Gson, not promised — the requirement should say that."

Then they rerun Step 7, and the refinement pass reads the existing REQUIREMENTS.md plus the hints file. It doesn't start from scratch — it treats the existing requirements as a baseline and focuses on the gaps the user identified. It backs up the current version first (REQUIREMENTS_before_refinement_1.md, _2.md, etc.) so they can diff and see exactly what changed.

The key constraints on the refinement pass:

It must not delete or weaken existing requirements. It can add new ones, sharpen conditions of satisfaction, split a vague requirement into more specific ones, or add missing alternative paths. But the baseline is additive-only unless the user explicitly says "REQ-047 is wrong, here's why."

It must report what it changed. A short summary at the end: "Added REQ-111 (deepCopy independence), sharpened REQ-088 (added specific escaped characters), split REQ-105 into REQ-105a/b for BigDecimal vs BigInteger." That makes the diff reviewable without comparing two 1,200-line files by hand.

And the hints file should be freeform. Don't make the user conform to a template — "I think you missed X" is enough. The model's job is to turn that into a concrete requirement using the same template as everything else. The user provides the domain insight, the pipeline provides the structure.

This also solves the reproducibility concern. Different models or different runs might miss different things. The hints file captures human judgment once, and subsequent runs incorporate it. Over time, recurring hints could migrate into the Step 4 domain checklist — if every project needs "deepCopy structural independence" called out, that becomes a permanent checklist item rather than a per-project hint.

Want me to build the refinement prompt and wire it into the pipeline?

## Assistant
*2026-04-04 12:40:27 UTC*

That's a really good design. The use cases become the navigation structure for the review, which means the user never reads a requirement out of context — they always see it through the lens of "what scenario does this govern?" That's exactly the top-down perspective you built the narrative pass to provide.

The dual-purpose REFINEMENT_HINTS.md is clean too. It tracks review progress (which use cases have been reviewed, which are pending) *and* accumulates feedback (things the user flagged during review). When the refinement pass runs, it reads the same file for both — it knows what was reviewed and what the reviewer said. And since it's a plain markdown file, the user can also just edit it directly if they want to add hints without going through the interactive flow.

A few thoughts on the mechanics:

The review prompt would read REQUIREMENTS.md, parse out the use cases and their linked REQ numbers, then check REFINEMENT_HINTS.md for progress state. If the file doesn't exist, everything is unreviewed. The prompt presents the menu — either "pick a use case" (self-guided) or "let's start with Use Case 1" (fully guided).

When drilling into a use case, the AI shows the use case description, then walks through each linked requirement — summary, conditions of satisfaction, alternative paths. For each one it asks: does this capture the right behavior? Is anything missing? Is anything wrong? The user can say "looks good," flag a gap ("this doesn't mention what happens when X is null"), or suggest a change. The AI writes that feedback to REFINEMENT_HINTS.md under the use case heading.

The progress format in REFINEMENT_HINTS.md could look like:

```
## Review Progress
- [x] Use Case 1: Serialize and deserialize a POJO — reviewed, no issues
- [x] Use Case 2: Register a custom type adapter — reviewed, see feedback below
- [ ] Use Case 3: Configure naming, exclusion, and strictness
- [ ] Use Case 4: Deserialize a generic type with TypeToken
...

## Feedback

### Use Case 2: Register a custom type adapter
- REQ-013: Missing condition of satisfaction for what happens when two 
  factories both claim the same type
- General: No requirement covers what happens when a registered adapter 
  throws during write — does the stream become corrupted?
```

That format is both human-readable and machine-parseable. The refinement pass can grep for unchecked items to know what wasn't reviewed, and read the feedback sections to know what to fix.

One thing worth noting: this also works across LLMs, like you said. The review session could be Claude, the refinement run could be GPT-5.4 via Copilot, the next review could be Gemini. REFINEMENT_HINTS.md is the handoff artifact — any model can read it and act on it. That's the same file-based-state principle that makes the whole pipeline work.

Want me to build the review prompt and the refinement prompt together? They're two halves of the same loop — review produces hints, refinement consumes them.

*2026-04-04 12:47:43 UTC*

```
This is close to complete. The sharpened domain checklist worked — every major gap I've been flagging across multiple iterations is now closed. Let me walk through what landed.

**Gaps now closed:**

REQ-069 (deepCopy) — finally present after being absent through every previous version. The conditions of satisfaction correctly distinguish mutable containers (must be independent copies) from immutable leaves (may return same instance). This was my most persistent gap item.

REQ-070 (identity contracts) — equals/hashCode/toString for all public tree types. The conditions correctly specify that JsonArray equality is order-sensitive, JsonObject equality is order-insensitive, and JsonPrimitive follows the numeric normalization rules. This is the kind of requirement nobody writes down until it breaks.

REQ-084 (arbitrary-precision and atomic adapters) — BigDecimal scale preservation, AtomicLong inheriting the configured long-as-string policy, atomic arrays maintaining index order. Exactly the built-in adapter contracts that were missing.

REQ-085 (textual scalar adapters) — URL, URI, UUID, Currency, Locale, InetAddress, each with their specific string form and quirks. The `"null"` string token backward compatibility for URL/URI is a detail that would catch a real regression.

REQ-092 (Calendar) — the six-field object shape with explicit field names and integer-range validation. Good addition.

REQ-102 (HTML escaping) — this is the strongest of the new requirements. It correctly identifies the three layers (base JSON escaping always on, HTML-safe escaping opt-in at writer level, Gson-managed writers default to HTML-safe), names the specific characters (`<`, `>`, `&`, `=`, `'`), and specifies the save/restore behavior when Gson temporarily overrides a caller-provided writer's setting. That's a testable, specific requirement that would catch a real bug.

**One concern about the completeness report.** It claims entry points are "COVERED" by REQ-007, REQ-008, REQ-100, REQ-101, REQ-102. But REQ-100 and REQ-101 are about pretty printing and FormattingStyle — those aren't entry point requirements. The domain checklist specifically asked for `JsonParser.parseString()`, `JsonParser.parseReader()`, `JsonStreamParser`, and `toJsonTree`/`fromJsonTree` as distinct paths. I don't see dedicated requirements for any of those. The completeness checker appears to have marked the entry points domain as covered without actually closing the gap.

`JsonStreamParser` in particular is a genuinely distinct entry point — it reads multiple JSON values from a single stream, which is a different contract from `Gson.fromJson()` that expects exactly one value. There's no requirement covering what happens when you iterate a `JsonStreamParser` and one of the values is malformed, or what the end-of-stream behavior is.

`toJsonTree` and `fromJsonTree` are also distinct — they produce or consume `JsonElement` trees instead of strings, and their null handling and type resolution behavior isn't necessarily identical to the string-based paths. REQ-007 (trailing data rejection) applies to string-based deserialization but may not apply the same way to tree-based deserialization.

Those are the only remaining gaps I can identify. Everything else is covered. 110 requirements with this quality of conditions of satisfaction, organized by use cases and cross-cutting concerns, with category narratives — this is a genuine specification document. If you handed it to a new engineer joining the Gson team, they would understand what the library promises and where the behavioral boundaries are. That's the goal.

The interactive review UX that Cowork designed — the use-case-guided walkthrough with progress tracking in REFINEMENT_HINTS.md — is the right way to catch these last few items. A user going through Use Case 5 (streaming API) would naturally ask "what about JsonStreamParser?" and the review session would capture that as a hint. That's the feedback loop working as designed.
```
